38th Street Gang.description: The 38th Street Gang is an American criminal street gang in Los Angeles, California, composed mainly of Mexican-Americans. Founded in the 1920s, it is one of the oldest street gangs in Los Angeles and is controlled by the Mexican Mafia. The gang engages in drug trafficking, assault, robbery, extortion, and other criminal activities. It gained notoriety during the 1942 Sleepy Lagoon murder trial and the subsequent Zoot Suit Riots.

descriptions: 38th Street gang is an ally of 18th Street.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Mexican Mafia, Sureños, 38th Street gang

descriptions: Loose alliance due to common rivals.
edges.evidence_quote: The gang also holds a loose alliance with the 18th Street Gang due to common rivals, mainly in South Los Angeles.

Chicanos Motorcycle Club.description: International outlaw motorcycle club founded in Pritzwalk, Germany on 23 August 2003. Acts as a support club for the Bandidos. Operates chapters in multiple countries including Germany, Netherlands, Norway, Russia, Serbia, Sweden, Ukraine, and UAE. Known for violent conflicts with Hells Angels and other groups.

descriptions: Infobox lists Chicanos as ally.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Brother Speed MC, Chicanos MC, Diablos MC, Mobshitters MC, Outlaws MC, Rock Machine MC, Satudarah MC, X-Team, Los Zetas

descriptions: Chicanos support Bandidos in Germany.
edges.evidence_quote: The German Bandidos are supported by the Chicanos, Diablos and X-Team.

descriptions: Bandidos are affiliated with Chicanos in Norway.
edges.evidence_quote: The Norwegian Bandidos are affiliated with the Chicanos and X-Team

descriptions: Chicanos are an affiliate club of Bandidos in Sweden.
edges.evidence_quote: Affiliates of the Bandidos in Sweden include the Chicanos , Diablos and X-Team .

descriptions: Chicanos are a support group of Bandidos.
edges.evidence_quote: The perpetrators were members of the Chicanos, a support group of the Bandidos motorcycle gang.

descriptions: Black Cobra and Chicanos were in conflict in Sweden in 2013.
edges.evidence_quote: In Sweden, the Black Cobra became involved in a conflict with the Chicanos , a Bandidos support club, after the Chicanos attempted to recruit Black Cobra members.

descriptions: Chicanos act as a support club for Bandidos.
edges.evidence_quote: The Chicanos are a support club (called puppet clubs by law enforcement) for the Bandidos.

descriptions: Chicanos and Black Cobra engaged in conflict and riot.
edges.evidence_quote: The Chicanos in Dalsland became involved in a conflict with the Black Cobra gang after attempting to recruit Black Cobra members. On 5 April 2013, members of the Chicanos and Black Cobra rioted in Trollhättan

descriptions: Brigade 81 attacked Chicanos clubhouse.
edges.evidence_quote: On 5 June 2009, members of the Hells Angels-affiliated Brigade 81 raided the Chicanos' clubhouse in Ludwigsfelde, leaving several club members injured.

descriptions: District 61 member shot at Chicanos members.
edges.evidence_quote: In June 2013, a member of District 61 – a group comprised partially of former Underdogs members – shot in self-defense at two Chicanos members in Halle city center, although nobody was wounded.

descriptions: Chicanos and Hells Angels engaged in violent clashes.
edges.evidence_quote: Chicanos members assaulted the president of the Hells Angels' nomads chapter in a nightclub in Eberswalde on 6 December 2008.

descriptions: No Surrender chapter patched over to become Chicanos.
edges.evidence_quote: Sweden's first Chicanos branch opened in Vänersborg in 2012, with a patch-over of a No Surrender chapter.

descriptions: Chicanos and Underdogs engaged in multiple violent confrontations.
edges.evidence_quote: Members of the Chicanos and the Underdogs Motorcycle Club fought in the street in Halle city center on 26 October 2010.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Chicanos MC

Velentzas Organization.description: Greek-American criminal organization operating in New York City, founded by Peter Kourakos in the 1950s. Under Spyredon Velentzas, it controlled illegal gambling operations in Astoria, Queens, and allied with the Lucchese crime family. After Velentzas' life sentence in 1992, the group declined but remains active under Fotios Dimopoulos.

descriptions: The Gambino family lists Velentzas Organization as a rival in the infobox.
edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Rudaj Organization, Velentzas Organization, and various other gangs in New York City, including their allies

descriptions: Lucchese family lists Velentzas Organization as an ally in the infobox.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Velentzas Organization

descriptions: Infobox lists Velentzas family as a rival of Rudaj Organization.
edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Lucchese crime family , Gambino crime family , Velentzas crime family

descriptions: The Gambino crime family opposed the Velentzas Organization's expansion into their gambling territory, leading to conflict.
edges.evidence_quote: Velentzas and his Greek crew attempted to expand their illegal gambling rackets further, but were met with resistance by the Gambino crime family. The FBI caught on wiretaps Gambino family boss John Gotti threatening to have Spiro killed for moving into the gambling territory controlled by the Gambino family.

descriptions: The Velentzas Organization originated from the Kourakos clan.
edges.evidence_quote: The group originated from the Kourakos clan, a Greek criminal organization led by Maniot Greek Peter Kourakos.

descriptions: The Velentzas Organization had a cooperative relationship with the Lucchese crime family, receiving protection and sharing profits.
edges.evidence_quote: Velentzas maintained a close working relationship with Lucchese crime family's Consigliere Christopher 'Christie Tick' Furnari. The Lucchese family provided protection to the Greeks, and received a portion of their gambling profits.

descriptions: The infobox lists the Philadelphia Greek Mob as an ally of the Velentzas Organization.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Lucchese crime family, Philadelphia Greek Mob

descriptions: The Rudaj Organization attacked members of the Velentzas Organization and took over their gambling operations, indicating conflict.
edges.evidence_quote: In June 2001, the Albanian Rudaj Organization began extorting Fotios Dimopoulos and attacked his associate Antonios Balampanis... The attack from the Rudaj organization was retaliation after a Greek member of the Velentzas family and Lucchese associate named Tony had reportedly broken some of their gambling machines.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivais: Comando Vermelho , UCA

Maniac Latin Disciples.description: Predominantly Hispanic street gang based in Chicago, Illinois, founded in 1966 as a Humboldt Park baseball club. They are part of the Folks Alliance and are involved in drug trafficking, assault, auto theft, robbery, homicide, money laundering, and weapons trafficking. The gang has an estimated 7,500-10,000 members and operates across multiple U.S. states.

Dixon Bloods.description: Street gang based in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, founded in 2006. Involved in drug and weapons trafficking, operating from a highrise complex on Dixon Road. Dismantled in 2013 after Project Traveller, with 43 members arrested. Still considered active.

descriptions: A former member of G-Shine Bloods allegedly co-founded the Dixon Bloods, indicating a structural link.
edges.evidence_quote: Unconfirmed rumours state that one of the founding members of the gang was a former member of a New York-based gang known as G-Shine Bloods, who had relocated to the neighborhood in the early 2000s.

descriptions: Bonde dos 40 has been violently battling PCM for control.
edges.evidence_quote: the group became known for instigating riots in prisons and in the Pedrinhas Penitentiary Complex against the Primeiro Comando do Maranhão (PCM), and for carrying out attacks on the streets, resulting in the deaths of over 100 inmates Since then, PCM and B40 have been violently battling for control over stilt neighborhoods and peripheral communities, causing dozens of deaths and injuries.

descriptions: B40 and PCM fought violently for control of neighborhoods.

Galatolo Mafia clan.description: The Galatolo Mafia clan was a criminal family originating from the Acquasanta neighborhood of Palermo, Sicily. One of the longest-running Mafia clans in the city, they held important positions throughout the 20th century and ruled the Resuttana mandamento in the early 21st century. They were involved in the violent Palermo wholesale market war in the 1950s and were devastated by internal betrayal. The clan was active from the 1950s until 2014.

descriptions: Galatolo clan fought Bontade clan over control of wholesale market racket.
edges.evidence_quote: The Acquasanta Mafia clan tried to muscle in on the protection racket that traditionally belonged the 'Mafia of the Gardens' — such as the Greco, Bontade and Sorci clans from the southeast of Palermo

descriptions: Cavataio faction betrayed and killed Galatolo leaders during the wholesale market war.
edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Cavataio Mafia clan (1950s)

descriptions: Galatolo clan allied with Corleonesi during the Second Mafia War.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Corleonesi (1980s)

descriptions: Galatolo clan fought Greco clan over control of wholesale market racket.
edges.evidence_quote: The Acquasanta Mafia clan tried to muscle in on the protection racket that traditionally belonged the 'Mafia of the Gardens' — such as the Greco, Bontade and Sorci clans from the southeast of Palermo

descriptions: Galatolo clan was subordinate to and allied with Madonia clan.
edges.evidence_quote: The Acquasanta family thus now answered to the Madonia clan, strong allies of the Galatolos.

descriptions: Galatolo clan ruled the Resuttana mandamento.
edges.evidence_quote: Vito Galatolo became the new leader of the Resuttana mandamento

descriptions: Galatolo clan fought Sorci clan over control of wholesale market racket.

edges.evidence_quote: Rivals: Acquasanta Mafia clan (Palermo wholesale market war in the mid 1950s)

Caltagirone Mafia family.description: The Caltagirone Mafia family is a Cosa Nostra crime family based in Caltagirone, Sicily, founded in 1982 by Francesco 'Ciccio' La Rocca. It engages in extortion, drug trafficking, and political manipulation. The family rose to prominence through alliances with the Catania Mafia and Corleonesi. Despite La Rocca's imprisonment and death in 2020, the family's influence persists.

descriptions: The Caltagirone Mafia allied with the Catania Mafia.
edges.evidence_quote: His alliances with powerful Cosa Nostra factions, particularly the Catania Mafia and the Corleonesi led by Totò Riina, further elevated the status of the Caltagirone Mafia clan within the Sicilian Mafia hierarchy.

descriptions: The Caltagirone Mafia was loyal to the Santapaola Mafia clan.
edges.evidence_quote: Despite longstanding loyalty to the Santapaola Mafia clan, a brief moment of internal dissent occurred during Ciccio’s imprisonment in the late 1990s.

descriptions: The Caltagirone Mafia allied with the Corleonesi.
edges.evidence_quote: His alliances with powerful Cosa Nostra factions, particularly the Catania Mafia and the Corleonesi led by Totò Riina, further elevated the status of the Caltagirone Mafia clan within the Sicilian Mafia hierarchy.

descriptions: The Caltagirone Mafia maintains ties with the Nardo clan.
edges.evidence_quote: The clan maintains ties with other criminal groups, including the Santapaola-Ercolano of Catania and the Nardo clan of Lentini.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Catania Mafia family , Ramacca Mafia family , Corleonesi Mafia clan

Popular Front for the Liberation of Pale.description: Palestinian Marxist-Leninist organization founded in 1967 by George Habash. It is the second-largest faction in the Palestine Liberation Organization, opposing Fatah's moderate stance and advocating a one-state solution. The group pioneered aircraft hijackings in the late 1960s and early 1970s and has been designated a terrorist organization by several countries. Its military wing is the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades.

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: The gang funded the PFLP through robberies.
edges.evidence_quote: Between December 1972 and May 1989, they committed robberies and sent the money to the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine.

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: The gang funded the PFLP through proceeds from robberies.
edges.evidence_quote: Between December 1972 and May 1989, they committed robberies and sent the money to the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine.

edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: The PKK lists the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine as an ally.
edges.evidence_quote: Allies: ... Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: ... PFLP

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades is the paramilitary wing of PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: The military wing of the PFLP is called the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades is the military wing of the PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: The military wing of the PFLP is called the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.target: Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: DFLP split from PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: In 1969, the Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine (DFLP) formed as a separate, ostensibly Maoist, organization under Nayef Hawatmeh and Yasser Abd Rabbo, initially as the PDFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP fights alongside Hamas in the Gaza war.
edges.evidence_quote: More recently, the group has participated in the ongoing Gaza war alongside Hamas and other allied Palestinian factions.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP's military wing fought alongside Hamas during the Gaza war (2023-present).
edges.evidence_quote: the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades published videos of it storming Israeli watchtowers during the October 7 attacks, and has since fought alongside Hamas and other allied factions in multiple battles inside the Gaza Strip.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP developed contacts with Hezbollah.
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP developed contacts at this time with Islamic fundamentalist groups linked to Iran – both Palestinian Hamas, and the Lebanon-based Hezbollah.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP developed ties with Japanese Red Army.
edges.evidence_quote: From its foundation, the PFLP sought superpower patrons, early on developing ties with the Soviet Union, the People's Republic of China, and, at various times, with regional powers such as Syria, South Yemen, Libya, North Korea, and Iraq, as well as with left-wing groups around the world, including the FARC and the Japanese Red Army.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Japanese Red Army collaborated with a PFLP faction in the Lod Airport massacre.
edges.evidence_quote: On 30 May 1972, 28 passengers were gunned down at Ben Gurion International Airport by members of the Japanese Red Army in collaboration with the PFLP's Waddie Haddad

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Palestinian Liberation Front merged into PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: Included within the discussions, as well as the ANM (including the RYO), were Heroes of Return (officially a collaboration with the PLO but actually under ANM control), Ahmed Jibril's Syria-based Palestinian Liberation Front, and a group of Jordanian Nasserists led by the former army officer Ahmed Za'rur. These groups combined to form the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine (PFLP) which was announced to the public on 11 December 1967.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PPSF split from PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: In 1967, Palestinian Popular Struggle Front (PPSF) broke away from the PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP-EO is a splinter faction of PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: The meeting was to evaluate the political relevance of cooperating with Wadi Haddad's PFLP-EO in addition to George Habash's PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine – General Command
descriptions: PFLP-GC split from PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: In October 1968, Ahmed Jibril led a break away from the PFLP to form the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine – General Command (PFLP-GC), and was joined by Ahmed Zarur and his followers.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP formed an alliance with Popular Resistance Committees.
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP has therefore formed alliances with other leftist groups formed within the Palestinian Authority, including the Palestinian People's Party and the Popular Resistance Committees of Gaza.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.target: Popular Revolutionary Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: Popular Revolutionary Front split from PFLP.
edges.evidence_quote: In 1972, the Popular Revolutionary Front for the Liberation of Palestine was formed following a split in PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP and RAF jointly planned the Landshut hijacking.
edges.evidence_quote: In 1977, Jensen may have staked out Palma de Mallorca Airport in preparation of the joint PFLP/RAF Landshut Hijacking

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP developed ties with FARC.
edges.evidence_quote: From its foundation, the PFLP sought superpower patrons, early on developing ties with the Soviet Union, the People's Republic of China, and, at various times, with regional powers such as Syria, South Yemen, Libya, North Korea, and Iraq, as well as with left-wing groups around the world, including the FARC and the Japanese Red Army.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
descriptions: PFLP and SLA clashed in South Lebanon, resulting in PFLP casualties.
edges.evidence_quote: On 8 June 1990 a clash occurred near Shuwayya in South Lebanon in which four members of the PFLP were killed by the South Lebanon Army (SLA).
